title: Pharma
subtitle: How EA for the Agentic Organisation applies to pharmaceuticals — an industry where agentic AI is transforming the entire value chain from drug discovery to manufacturing.
description: The pharmaceutical industry faces unique EA challenges: GxP validation requirements for every system change, multi-modal data spanning genomics through manufacturing, 10–15 year drug development lifecycles, extreme IP sensitivity, and a triple regulatory overlay (FDA AI guidance, EU GMP Annex 22, EU AI Act). 73% of global pharma organisations are actively deploying or piloting agentic AI.

findings.title: 73% of pharma organisations are deploying or piloting agentic AI
findings.summary: 80% are investing specifically in agentic AI capabilities, with 79% investing in intelligent manufacturing. Investment reached ~$4 billion in 2025, projected to exceed $25 billion annually by 2030. However, over 40% of agentic AI initiatives may be cancelled by 2027 without clear business value and governance.

findings.title: AI-designed drugs are now in human trials
findings.summary: Insilico Medicine's AI-discovered ISM001-055 achieved positive Phase IIa results. Helixon signed a $1.7 billion deal with Sanofi for AI-designed antibodies. Multiple Phase III readouts expected throughout 2026 — the first true test of whether AI can deliver drugs that work at scale.

findings.title: Triple regulatory overlay creates unique GxP architecture requirements
findings.summary: FDA's Good AI Practice principles (January 2026), EU GMP Annex 22 (draft), and the EU AI Act impose overlapping compliance requirements. AI systems informing safety, quality, or manufacturing decisions must now meet device-level validation. Architecture must separate GxP-validated AI from non-GxP AI.

findings.title: GxP-stratified architecture is the defining challenge
findings.summary: Manufacturing and quality AI must be validated under 21 CFR 211 and EU GMP rules. Discovery and commercial AI can operate under lighter governance. EA must define clear boundaries, validation frameworks, and change control processes for each stratum.

findings.title: SAP ECC end-of-life forces $2M–$1B migration decisions
findings.summary: Only 39% of SAP ECC customers had migrated to S/4HANA by late 2024, with mainstream support ending in 2027. Migration projects take 3–7 years. Pharma faces additional complexity from GxP validation requirements for every system change.

findings.title: Palantir’s Ontology approach is extending into life sciences
findings.summary: Parexel (clinical research), Velocity Clinical Research (trial payments), Merck, and Sanofi are deploying Palantir platforms. The Ontology maps pharma business objects (compounds, trials, patients, batches) across R&D, manufacturing, and commercial, enabling AI agents to reason about domain concepts.

findings.title: Clinical trials are being fundamentally re-architected
findings.summary: 40%+ of companies are innovating in decentralised trials or real-world evidence generation. AI powers protocol generation, patient matching, site selection, digital twins, and synthetic control arms. AstraZeneca is embedding AI throughout the entire trial lifecycle.

findings.title: Roche built the industry’s largest clinical genomic database
findings.summary: 800,000+ genomic profiles across 150+ tumour subtypes via Flatiron Health and Foundation Medicine acquisitions. Roche’s ‘lab in a loop’ strategy iterates AI models with lab experiments, targeting 50% efficiency gains in safety management by 2026.

case_studies.description: AstraZeneca has integrated the ChatInvent agentic system directly into its discovery pipeline for molecular design and synthesis planning. Generative AI is being scaled across the entire development lifecycle to support the company's ambition to deliver 20 new medicines and reach $80 billion in revenue. In mid-2025, AstraZeneca struck a $5 billion+ deal with CSPC to access an AI-discovered oncology portfolio.

Roche’s ‘lab in a loop’ strategy iterates AI models with laboratory experiments in continuous feedback cycles. Through acquisitions of Flatiron Health and Foundation Medicine, Roche built the industry’s largest clinical genomic database — over 800,000 profiles across 150+ tumour subtypes — targeting 50% efficiency gains in safety management by 2026.

Novartis has embedded AI into its dedicated AI Innovation Lab, leveraging partnerships with Microsoft for scalable ML frameworks and with Isomorphic Labs (founded by Nobel laureate Demis Hassabis) for AI-driven drug discovery. The approach combines large-scale computational chemistry with clinical and real-world data for end-to-end discovery acceleration.

traditional.summary: Drug discovery, clinical trials, manufacturing, and supply chain are managed through sequential, heavily manual processes with long cycle times.
traditional.body_html:
Drug Discovery

Target identification through literature review and experimental screening. Molecule design by medicinal chemists iterating on structure-activity relationships. Lead optimisation through manual synthesis and testing cycles. Typical timeline: 4–6 years from target to clinical candidate.

Clinical Trials

Protocol design by clinical teams over weeks. Patient recruitment through site networks. Manual monitoring and adverse event reporting. Regulatory submissions compiled by specialists. Typical Phase I–III duration: 6–10 years.

Manufacturing

Batch-based production with periodic quality testing. Manual process documentation. Preventive maintenance on fixed schedules. Quality investigations after deviations.

Supply Chain

Demand forecasting based on historical trends. Cold chain monitoring with manual alerts. Serialisation compliance through dedicated systems. Limited end-to-end visibility across global supply networks.

agentic.summary: <mark>AI agents are transforming the entire pharma value chain</mark> — from autonomous molecular design through AI-powered clinical trials to intelligent manufacturing and predictive supply chains.
agentic.body_html: 73% of pharma organisations are actively deploying or piloting agentic AI, with investment reaching ~$4 billion in 2025. AI-designed drugs are now in human trials. The shift is from sequential, manual processes to agent-orchestrated workflows with human governance at critical decision points.
key_points: Discovery agents autonomously design molecules, plan synthesis routes, and predict properties — AstraZeneca's ChatInvent is in production for molecular design
Clinical trial agents generate protocols, match patients, select sites, create digital twins, and draft regulatory submissions
Manufacturing agents provide real-time quality control, continuous process verification, and predictive maintenance — 79% of pharma investing in intelligent manufacturing
Supply chain agents forecast demand using epidemiological and real-world signals, monitor cold chains predictively, and manage serialisation compliance
Pharmacovigilance agents detect adverse events, manage safety signals, and support post-market surveillance across global markets

traditional.summary: Regulatory compliance managed through Computer System Validation (CSV), periodic audits, and dedicated quality and regulatory affairs teams.
traditional.body_html:
GxP Compliance

Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP), Good Clinical Practice (GCP), Good Laboratory Practice (GLP), and Good Pharmacovigilance Practice (GVP) govern all aspects of drug development and production. Computer System Validation (CSV) required for every system touching GxP data. 21 CFR Part 11 governs electronic records and signatures.

Regulatory Submissions

Manual compilation of regulatory dossiers (CTD format). Submission to FDA, EMA, and regional authorities. Post-market surveillance and periodic safety update reports.

Quality Management

Quality Management Systems (QMS) with documented SOPs, CAPA processes, and change control. ALCOA+ principles (Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original, Accurate) for data integrity.

agentic.summary: A <mark>triple regulatory overlay</mark> — FDA AI guidance, EU GMP Annex 22, and EU AI Act — creates unique GxP architecture requirements for pharma.
agentic.body_html: The FDA published 'Guiding Principles of Good AI Practice in Drug Development' in January 2026, mandating that AI systems informing safety, quality, or manufacturing decisions meet device-level validation. The EU's draft GMP Annex 22 provides detailed guidance on AI in manufacturing. The EU AI Act classifies AI in medical devices and clinical decisions as high-risk. All three frameworks demand traceability, explainability, and human oversight.
key_points: Architecture must separate GxP-validated AI (manufacturing, quality, clinical) from non-GxP AI (discovery, commercial) with clear governance boundaries
Every AI change in GxP environments must follow validated change control with documented SOPs and quality records
ALCOA+ data integrity principles must extend to all AI-generated outputs used in regulatory submissions or quality decisions
EU AI Act high-risk obligations for clinical decision support effective August 2026 — architecture must embed compliance controls now

traditional.summary: A binary GxP/non-GxP system classification with Computer System Validation applied uniformly to all regulated systems.
traditional.body_html:
Current Approach

Systems classified as either GxP or non-GxP. GxP systems undergo full Computer System Validation (CSV) lifecycle: validation plan, requirements specification, design specification, testing protocols (IQ/OQ/PQ), validation report. Every change requires formal change control with impact assessment and re-validation.

Challenges

CSV processes designed for stable, deterministic software. Validation cycles of 6–18 months incompatible with AI model iteration speed. Binary classification doesn't account for the spectrum of AI risk levels. Traditional CSV cannot validate non-deterministic AI/ML outputs.

agentic.summary: Architecture must support a <mark>spectrum of validation levels</mark> from fully validated GxP AI in manufacturing to lightweight governance for discovery exploration.
agentic.body_html: The defining architectural challenge for pharma is that GxP-validated AI (deterministic, heavily regulated, slow-changing) and experimental AI (probabilistic, fast-evolving, agentic) must coexist. Discovery agents need rapid iteration; manufacturing agents need validated stability. The EU's GMP Annex 22 draft emphasises static, deterministic models for GMP with human oversight, while discovery AI operates under a fundamentally different paradigm.
key_points: Multi-tier validation framework: Tier 1 (full GxP validation for manufacturing/quality), Tier 2 (risk-based validation for clinical), Tier 3 (lightweight governance for discovery/commercial)
Promotion gates between tiers — a discovery AI model must pass formal validation before entering clinical or manufacturing use
Separate environments with controlled interfaces: discovery sandbox, clinical staging, manufacturing validated, commercial governed
Continuous validation approaches emerging as alternative to traditional CSV for AI/ML systems that evolve over time

traditional.summary: Fragmented systems landscape spanning R&D, clinical, manufacturing, quality, regulatory, and commercial with limited cross-functional data sharing.
traditional.body_html:
Core Systems

Laboratory Information Management Systems (LIMS). Electronic Lab Notebooks (ELN). Clinical Data Management Systems (CDMS). Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES). Enterprise Resource Planning (SAP ECC/S4HANA). Quality Management Systems (QMS). Regulatory Information Management Systems (RIMS). Pharmacovigilance databases.

Integration

Siloed systems with limited cross-functional data flow. Data duplication across R&D, manufacturing, and commercial. No unified semantic model. SAP ECC end-of-life 2027 forcing major migration decisions. GxP validation requirements slow every integration change.

agentic.summary: An <mark>Ontology-based semantic layer</mark> connecting R&D, clinical, manufacturing, and commercial data — enabling AI agents to reason across the entire drug lifecycle.
agentic.body_html: Palantir's deployments at Parexel, Merck, and Sanofi demonstrate a platform pattern where a semantic Ontology layer maps pharma business objects (compounds, targets, trials, patients, batches, regulatory submissions) to underlying data across all systems. AI agents reason about domain concepts rather than raw tables. The multi-modal data challenge (genomic, clinical, manufacturing, commercial) is uniquely complex in pharma.
key_points: Ontology-first approach: model drug lifecycle objects semantically before deploying AI agents across the value chain
Multi-modal data fabric: unifying genomic, proteomic, clinical, manufacturing, and commercial data with full lineage tracking
GxP-aware platform: the semantic layer must respect validation boundaries — manufacturing data governed differently from discovery data
SAP S/4HANA migration as the foundation for AI-ready manufacturing and supply chain (only 39% migrated as of late 2024)
Architecture must protect patent-critical IP — AI agents handling molecular data require extreme access controls

traditional.summary: Sequential clinical trial design with manual protocol writing, site-based recruitment, and paper-heavy regulatory submissions.
traditional.body_html:
Trial Design

Protocol development by clinical teams over weeks. Fixed-design trials with predetermined endpoints. Site-based patient recruitment through investigator networks. Manual monitoring and source data verification.

Regulatory Submissions

Manual compilation of Common Technical Document (CTD) format submissions. Specialist teams for each section. Review cycles measured in months. Post-market surveillance through manual adverse event reporting.

Real-World Evidence

Emerging use of claims data and EHR data for observational studies. Limited integration with clinical trial data. Manual literature review for pharmacovigilance signals.

agentic.summary: Clinical trials are being <mark>fundamentally re-architected with AI-powered design, execution, and evidence generation</mark>.
agentic.body_html: 40%+ of companies are innovating in decentralised trials or real-world evidence generation. AstraZeneca is embedding AI throughout the entire trial lifecycle. AI-powered digital twins and synthetic control arms are reducing the need for traditional comparator groups. CDER is seeing a significant increase in AI-component regulatory submissions.
key_points: AI protocol generation: agents create comprehensive study protocols, simulate trial arms, and optimise designs for regulatory acceptance
Digital twins & synthetic controls: AI-generated virtual patient populations reducing comparator group sizes and accelerating timelines
Decentralised trials: remote monitoring, wearable data collection, and AI-powered patient engagement across geographies
Automated regulatory submissions: agents drafting, validating, and assembling CTD-format submissions with compliance checking
Roche's genomic database: 800K+ profiles powering precision medicine trial design and real-world evidence generation

traditional.summary: SAP ECC approaching end-of-life in 2027, with only 39% of customers migrated and pharma-specific GxP constraints adding years to timelines.
traditional.body_html:
Scale of the Challenge

SAP ECC mainstream support ends 2027. Only 39% of customers migrated to S/4HANA by late 2024. Migration projects: 3–7 years, $2M to $1B. Pharma-specific: every system change in GxP environments requires formal validation. 50% of on-premises customised applications projected to modernise by 2026.

Data Challenges

Systems not designed for today's data volumes or real-time requirements. DSCSA serialisation and EU Falsified Medicines Directive compliance require real-time data. ALCOA+ data integrity principles constrain migration approaches. Legacy data mapping and cleansing is the most time-consuming activity.

agentic.summary: Agentic AI can <mark>accelerate pharma legacy modernisation</mark> while being uniquely constrained by GxP validation requirements for every system change.
agentic.body_html: The pharma modernisation paradox is more acute than in banking or insurance: AI agents need clean, integrated data, but GxP validation requirements mean every system change (including data migration) must be formally validated. This makes the strangler-fig pattern more attractive than big-bang replacement. AI agents can assist with code migration, test generation, and data quality remediation — but must themselves be validated before touching GxP systems.
key_points: GxP-constrained migration: every data migration step requires formal validation, adding months to timelines that are already 3–7 years
Strangler-fig pattern preferred: new AI capabilities deployed alongside legacy, gradually taking over processes with validated interfaces
AI-assisted migration: agents can generate test scripts, map data, and identify quality issues — but must be validated for GxP use
SAP S/4HANA + BTP as hybrid landscape: some components cloud, some on-premises, all requiring GxP-compliant integration
